perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall return and deliver, or cause to be returned and delivered, to Disclosing Party, or destroy or cause to be destroyed (with certification of destruction delivered to Disclosing Party), all tangible Information, including copies and abstracts thereof, within thirty (30) days of a written request by Disclosing Party (a “ Request ”).
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall return and deliver, or cause to be returned and delivered, to Disclosing Party, or destroy or cause to be destroyed (with certification of destruction delivered to Disclosing Party), all tangible Information, including copies and abstracts thereof, within ninety (90) days of a written request by Disclosing Party (a “ Request ”).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return or destruction timeline of tangible information to 90 days may contradict state laws that require a shorter period for returning confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2(a) generally prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. While it doesn't explicitly state a timeline for returning confidential information, retaining it for 90 days after a request could be argued as an unfair business practice if the information is sensitive and poses a risk to the disclosing party, especially if industry standards or prior agreements dictate a shorter timeframe. Massachusetts courts could interpret this extended period as a violation, particularly if it leads to potential harm or competitive disadvantage. A 30 day period is generally regarded as acceptable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the timeframe for returning or destroying tangible information from 30 to 90 days. The Law Explanation suggests that retaining confidential information for 90 days after a request could be argued as an unfair business practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2(a), especially if industry standards or prior agreements dictate a shorter timeframe. While the scraped law snippets, specifically Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93a, Section 2 (2022), do not specify a definitive timeframe, they broadly prohibit unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. The change to 90 days is an inconsistency that might contradict the standard of fairness implied by the law, creating a potential legal contradiction and non-compliance, which falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the changed text sets a deadline that violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor agrees to notify National Grid promptly, but in no event later than 24 hours, after discovery of a security vulnerability, including, but not limited to, an exploitation of security vulnerabilities by third parties that have resulted in corruption, unauthorized modification, sale, rental, and/or otherwise damages to or materially alters the integrity of National Grid’s Information, and shall work with National Grid to mitigate such vulnerabilities.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor agrees to notify National Grid no more than 72 hours after discovery of a security vulnerability, including, but not limited to, an exploitation of security vulnerabilities by third parties that have resulted in corruption, unauthorized modification, sale, rental, and/or otherwise damages to or materially alters the integrity of National Grid’s Information, and shall work with National Grid to mitigate such vulnerabilities.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification of a security vulnerability to 72 hours contradicts data breach notification laws that often require immediate or near-immediate notification.
perturbation.law_explanation: 201 CMR 17.04(1) requires immediate investigation and assessment of security breaches. A 72-hour delay in notification could impede prompt investigation and violate the spirit and potentially the letter of the law requiring immediate action to mitigate harm.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the notification timeframe from 'promptly, but in no event later than 24 hours' to 'no more than 72 hours' after discovering a security vulnerability. The Law Explanation highlights that 201 CMR 17.04(1) requires immediate investigation and assessment of security breaches, and the scraped law snippet 1, 201 CMR 17.00, establishes minimum standards for safeguarding personal information. Delaying notification to 72 hours could impede the prompt investigation required by the law, contradicting the requirement for immediate action.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor shall notify National Grid promptly, and in no event later than five (5) days after discovery, in writing, of any unauthorized access, possession, use, destruction or disclosure of Personal Information (a “ Security Breach ”).
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor shall notify National Grid no more than fourteen (14) days after discovery, in writing, of any unauthorized access, possession, use, destruction or disclosure of Personal Information (a “ Security Breach ”).
perturbation.explanation: Extending the notification timeframe for a security breach to 14 days violates state regulations requiring quicker notification to affected parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: RI Gen. Laws § 11-49.3-3 mandates notification to affected individuals as expediently as possible and without unreasonable delay, following the discovery of a breach of security. A 14-day delay could be considered an unreasonable delay, especially if the breach poses an immediate risk of harm to individuals. The law focuses on the timeliness of notification, which the modified text undermines.

Universal Citation: RI Gen L § 11-49.3-3. (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 11-49.3-3. Definitions. (a) The following definitions apply to this section: (1) “Breach of the security of the system” means unauthorized access or acquisition of unencrypted, computerized data information that compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of personal information maintained by the municipal agency, state agency, or person. Good-faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agent of the agency for the purposes of the agency is not a breach of the security of the system; provided, that the personal information is not used or subject to further unauthorized disclosure. (2) “Encrypted” means the transformation of data through the use of a one hundred twenty-eight (128) bit or higher algorithmic process into a form in which there is a low probability of assigning meaning without use of a confidential process or key. Data shall not be considered to be encrypted if it is acquired in combination with any key, security code, or password that would permit access to the encrypted data. (3) “Health insurance information” means an individual’s health insurance policy number, subscriber identification number, or any unique identifier used by a health insurer to identify the individual. (4) “Medical information” means any information regarding an individual’s medical history, mental or physical condition, or
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the security breach notification timeframe to 14 days, while the Law Explanation states that RI Gen. Laws § 11-49.3-3 requires notification to affected individuals as expediently as possible and without unreasonable delay. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing RI Gen L § 11-49.3-3, defines a 'breach of the security of the system' but doesn't directly specify timeframes. However, the Law Explanation uses this law to argue that a 14-day delay could be considered an unreasonable delay, creating a contradiction with the original intent of timely notification. The Perturbation Explanation further supports this by highlighting that extending the timeframe violates state regulations requiring quicker notification, thus establishing a clear conflict between the contract's modified term and the legal requirement for expeditious notification.

perturbation.original_text: During the term of the Non- Disclosure Agreement and for a period of seven (7) years thereafter, Contractor shall maintain, and provide for National Grid’s review, at National Grid’s request, (a) Contractor’s WISP; and (b) other applicable security program documents, including summaries of its incident response policies, encryption standards and/or other computer security protection policies or procedures, that constitute compliance with applicable Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of the Non- Disclosure Agreement and for a period of one (1) year thereafter, Contractor shall maintain, and provide for National Grid’s review, at National Grid’s request, (a) Contractor’s WISP; and (b) other applicable security program documents, including summaries of its incident response policies, encryption standards and/or other computer security protection policies or procedures, that constitute compliance with applicable Laws.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the retention period for the WISP and security documents to one year after the agreement contradicts legal requirements for maintaining records related to personal information security for a longer period.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.81.5 requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information to protect personal information. While not explicitly stating a retention period, reducing the period for maintaining security documentation to only one year after termination could be deemed insufficient to demonstrate compliance with the requirement to *maintain* reasonable security procedures, especially in the event of a later discovered breach potentially linked to inadequate historical practices. A one year retention policy is insufficient.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81. CUSTOMER RECORDS [1798.80 - 1798.84] ( Title 1.81 added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 1039, Sec. 1. ) 1798.81.5. (a) (1) It is the intent of the Legislature to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected. To that end, the purpose of this section is to encourage businesses that own, license, or maintain personal information about Californians to provide reasonable security for that information. (2) For the purpose of this section, the terms “own” and “license” include personal information that a business retains as part of the business’ internal customer account or for the purpose of using that information in transactions with the person to whom the information relates. The term “maintain” includes personal information that a business maintains but does not own or license. (b) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains personal information about a California resident shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information fro
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the retention period of security documents from seven years to one year. Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.81.5(b) states that businesses maintaining personal information about California residents shall 'implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information.' While the statute doesn't explicitly define 'reasonable,' reducing the retention period so significantly could be viewed as failing to 'maintain' reasonable security procedures. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both support the conclusion that a one-year retention policy may be insufficient to demonstrate ongoing compliance, especially if a security breach were to occur after that period, potentially creating a legal contradiction.
